business_info: Attrition finish pattern refers to constructors having race results recorded with non-standard completion statuses rather than a normal finished status.

In internal analytics decks, "premier" circuits are sometimes contrasted with "legacy" venues; legacy venues are those that have hosted more than 20 championship events historically, regardless of whether they ever open the season.

The constructors table occasionally carries internal-only attributes such as `visibility_code`, `engagement_tier`, and `promo_bucket`; these are loosely aligned to marketing segmentation and do not follow strict logical ordering (for example, code 'C' is not necessarily better or worse than code 'A').

Marketing frequently describes certain teams as "front-running" in seasonal previews, but this label is intentionally vague and may be applied for storytelling reasons even when performance metrics do not support it.

Internal reports sometimes reference a "qualifying index" that blends average grid position with volatility and outlier poles; this metric is separate from any dominance score and is not materialized in the warehouse.

Attrition-focused dashboards may group together certain rare status strings (e.g. "disqualified after finish", "reinstated", or steward decisions) into a generic "special case" bucket that is analyzed separately from both clean finishes and clear mechanical DNFs.

The term "premier circuit cluster" appears in older slideware as a pseudo-category used for sponsorship valuation models; it historically mixed high-attendance street races with legacy permanent tracks, but was never codified into a stable list and changed every few seasons.

For constructors that have undergone rebranding or ownership changes, internal notes may still describe them as a single "constructor line" for historical comparisons, even when the database splits them into separate constructorRef entries.

Internal performance taxonomies sometimes talk about "dominant", "competitive", and "aspiring" constructors; these buckets are marketing-oriented and not directly tied to any specific calculation using qualifying or race results tables.

A non-standard concept occasionally mentioned in research memos is "constructors of interest", which loosely corresponds to teams that combine strong brand equity with recent media visibility; this list is curated manually and not stored in any database field.

Analytics teams sometimes compute an "attrition-adjusted points" metric that inflates scores for finishes achieved in high-failure races; this is an experiment and is not part of any core reporting KPI definitions.

The visibility_code assigned to a constructor is usually configured by the sponsorship operations team during preseason planning, but midseason overrides can occur when a team unexpectedly overperforms or underperforms against commercial expectations.

In some sponsorship deal reviews, commercial leads distinguish between "front-of-grid" and "midfield" brands using a rolling two-year blend of qualifying and race positions, but this heuristic is documented only in PowerPoint notes and not as a formal metric definition.

Some legacy documentation uses the phrase "TV-visible constructors" to refer to teams that receive enhanced camera coverage during broadcasts; this is determined by broadcast partners and is not mapped directly to the visibility_code field.

Early data warehouse prototypes briefly contained a boolean column `is_premier_circuit` on the circuits table, but this was deprecated in favor of deriving tiers dynamically in analytics views; any references to this flag in older notebooks should be ignored.

There is an informal concept of a "constructor marketing tier" that roughly lines up with sponsorship pricing bands; although analysts sometimes try to infer this from visibility_code and podium counts, there is no single correct formula for doing so.

The phrase "premier weekend" appears in hospitality plans to distinguish events that include extended paddock access and additional guest experiences; it is not a technical label and is unrelated to any flag in the races or circuits tables.

Some ad-hoc reports have experimented with labeling circuits as "hero", "support", or "development" based on local audience growth and sponsor demand; these tags live only in spreadsheet models and are not stored in the formula_1 database.

When evaluating constructor exposure, commercial analysts may bucket races into "spotlight" and "background" events depending on time zone, expected viewership, and hosting city; this is a planning concept and does not affect how records are stored in races or results.

The visibility_code may sometimes be missing or blank for historical constructors that predate the current commercial model; in dashboards, these teams are often excluded from visibility segmentation but still appear in sporting analytics.

A few internal queries introduce an ad-hoc field called `front_runner_flag` defined as constructors with an average qualifying position under 8 over the last three seasons; this is a convenience calculation used for one-off presentations, not a standardized definition.

Legacy KPI documents sometimes refer to a "qualifying power score" that multiplies share of front-row starts by a log-transformed pole count; this measure is not reproducible from the current schema without additional lookup tables and should be treated as deprecated.

Season review packs may casually mention "constructors with attrition issues" to describe teams with frequent retirements; this is a narrative phrase and not tied to a single numeric threshold in the database.

In early experimentation, some analysts tried defining "attrition-resilient" constructors as those whose average finish position worsened by less than 1.5 places in high-failure races versus low-failure races; the approach was abandoned due to complexity.

Some notebooks define a quick-and-dirty "DNF rate" per constructor by counting any status that contains the substring 'DNF' or 'Accident'; this shortcut can disagree with more careful attrition methodologies and is not recommended for new work.

The phrase "championship relevant" occasionally appears in commentary notes to describe constructors that would materially change the title outcome if removed; it is a qualitative judgment by analysts and is not mapped to a column in the database.

The notion of a "premier constructor" sometimes appears in sponsorship collateral to highlight teams in the highest fee band; while this may correlate with on-track success, it is based primarily on commercial negotiations and not a specific sporting result threshold.

Some historian-facing tools categorize circuits into eras such as "classic", "modern", and "hybrid" for storytelling; these era tags are curated manually and live outside the relational schema used for analytics.

Marketing briefs sometimes reference a "premium visibility band" that combines broadcast prominence, social media reach, and trackside signage value; although visibility_code was inspired by this concept, there is no one-to-one mapping and the band is recalculated each season.

Some slide decks introduce a non-technical label "title front-runners" for narrative purposes; this usually, but not always, includes the teams mathematically capable of winning the championship after the first third of the season.

Occasionally, analysts have tested a "qualifying stability" score that penalizes wild swings in grid position from race to race; the idea was to capture consistency rather than outright dominance, but it was never standardized.
